name: Cart and Wagon
tagline: Four-wheeled and two-wheeled vehicles that multiply land freight using wheels, axles, and draft animals or humans
problem:
Human backs scale poorly with grain surpluses, timber lengths, and stone blocks. Sledges work but waste energy ploughing soil; pack animals cap out at modest loads per animal. The wheeled vehicle problem is distributing weight across multiple rolling contacts, steering a front axle or pivot, and braking descent without destroying wheels.

Two-wheeled carts tip easily but track narrow paths; four-wheeled wagons need steering geometry — either pivoting front axle or turntable — or they scrub tires and break on turns. Suspension is historically minimal; road quality dominates ride.

Without standardised hubs, axle sizes, and repairable parts, vehicle fleets cannot be maintained at civilisation scale. Thus carts are not only carpentry but logistics standards.
overview:
A basic cart pairs a chassis (bed) with an undercarriage: axles, wheels, and often a drawbar for animal harness. Wheels may be fixed to the axle (both rotate) or rotate on fixed axles with pins — the latter wears journals and needs grease.

Steering for four-wheelers historically uses a pivoting front assembly or side-by-side animals with a flexible pole. Braking uses drag shoes, rope wraps on wheel rims, or later band brakes — absent brakes, loads control descent with chocks and human ropes.

Materials: hardwood frames, iron tyres shrunk on rims, steel leaf springs later. Tyre contact patch and ground pressure determine whether the vehicle ruts mud or floats on grass.

Human traction variants exist — handcarts — obeying the same load paths with different ergonomic constraints.

In collapse recovery, cart and wagon must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Ground pressure
principles.explanation: Load divided by contact area predicts sinkage in soft soils — wider rims or more wheels reduce rutting.

principles.name: Scrub and Ackermann (conceptual)
principles.explanation: Turning geometry ideally arcs concentric circles for each wheel; rigid axles without differential scrub on tight turns, increasing wear and draft effort.

principles.name: Centre of mass height
principles.explanation: High stacks reduce rollover stability on cambered roads — lateral acceleration limits scale with track width over height.

principles.name: Draft line alignment
principles.explanation: Harness attachment height changes animal biomechanics and tongue loading on the chassis — misalignment causes chronic animal injury and frame fatigue.

components.name: Bed / platform
components.function: Supports cargo; may include removable side boards
components.criticalNote: Grain direction across span resists bending better than along short dimension

components.name: Axle assembly
components.function: Transfers load to wheels; may transmit brake torque
components.madeFrom: Wrought iron or steel later; wood in light carts
components.criticalNote: Bending moment peaks at centre — avoid midspan welds without reinforcement

components.name: Wheels
components.criticalNote: Nut retention on axles — castellated nuts and cotter pins after metal age

components.name: Steering pivot (four-wheel)
components.function: Allows front axle to turn relative to frame
components.criticalNote: Grease schedule — dry pins ovalise under shock

components.name: Drawbar and harness attachment
components.function: Applies tractive force without twisting frame
components.position: Forward of front axle or centre pivot
components.madeFrom: Forged iron with safety chain secondaries
components.criticalNote: Safety chain prevents runaway if primary coupling fails

rawMaterials.name: Iron fastenings
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Hot rivet or forge weld straps

buildSteps.title: Engineer load case
buildSteps.description: Estimate worst-case mass and terrain camber. Choose wheel count and track width from ground pressure targets.
buildSteps.warningNote: Dynamic loads from potholes exceed static weight — use safety factor ≥2 for rural roads

buildSteps.title: Fabricate bed and crossmembers
buildSteps.description: Mortise and tenon or bolted joints; install cross sills directly over axle seats to transfer load vertically.
buildSteps.warningNote: End-grain bearing on bolts crushes — use washers and hardwood

buildSteps.title: Mount axles and wheels
buildSteps.description: Set caster and toe for steerable front if applicable; ream bearing bores; retain with cotters.
buildSteps.warningNote: Spin test — listen for rub; shim cheek plates square

buildSteps.title: Attach drawgear
buildSteps.description: Align tongue height to expected harness; install safety chains with correct working angle — not so long they drag, not so short they bind turns.
buildSteps.warningNote: Never stand in line with chain recoil path during first pulls

buildSteps.title: Load test and document
buildSteps.description: Progressive loading with known masses; inspect axle deflection. Paint load limit on bed rail.
buildSteps.warningNote: Brake test on mild slope before mountain use

history.event: Early wagon burials and models document four-wheeled transport in Old World contexts.
Standardised vehicle components and road networks synergise long-distance bulk movement.

impact:
Carts convert agricultural surplus into mobile calories — enabling markets, armies, and migrations. They also damage roads without maintenance, creating co-evolution with road engineering.

Modern descendants include trailers and container chassis — same load path thinking at megatonne scale.

sources.title: NYC DOT — Brooklyn Bridge (historic infrastructure case study)
sources.note: Public works engineering and load-carrying infrastructure context

sources.title: FAO — Home
sources.note: Draught animal welfare, rural transport, and agricultural logistics (verify regional guidance)
